text: The surgeon makes a skin incision approximately three millimeters long along the anterior axillary line, two centimeters above the iliac crest. A disposable cannula is used to infiltrate the subcutaneous tissue with a modified solution. After a fifteen-minute wait, liposuction is performed using a 13-gauge cannula connected to a vac-lock syringe, targeting the subcutaneous adipose tissue of the anterior abdominal wall.

The aspirated adipose tissue is transferred to a 10-milliliter syringe and then into a cylinder containing saline solution and metallic spheres. The cylinder, equipped with blue and gray filters, is shaken until the saline solution becomes clear, indicating emulsification and microfracturing of the adipose tissue. The processed tissue flows through the gray filter into a syringe, and is subsequently transferred into several 1-milliliter syringes with a 22-gauge needle for injection.

A three-dimensional transanal ultrasound reveals the anal sphincter gap between nine and twelve o'clock. The surgeon assesses the defect manually and performs an injection at the sphincter defect using a one cc syringe under ultrasound guidance. During withdrawal, a two-dimensional transanal ultrasound shows a hyper-echoic image at nine to ten o'clock, indicating the deposit of the injected product.

The surgeon continues the infiltration of the final product anteriorly to fill the anal sphincter gap, completing the injection along the entire length of the anal canal. Approximately 30-32 milliliters of the product are injected, resulting in a visible bulking effect that can be appreciated by palpation.
The surgeon checks the integrity of the mucosa of the anal canal and the vagina. The transanal ultrasound post-treatment reveals hyperechoic deposits at the sphincter gap.
